items.title: VDC vs. RSPS: Broad Diversification or Balanced Bets for Consumer Staples Investors?
items.author: Sara Appino
items.description: Vanguard's VDC consumer staples ETF outperforms Invesco's RSPS with lower fees (0.09% vs 0.40%), broader diversification (105 vs 37 holdings), and stronger 1-year returns (0.05% vs -3.2%). VDC uses market-cap weighting with megacap concentration, while RSPS employs equal-weighting to reduce single-stock risk. Both offer defensive sector exposure with lower volatility, appealing to investors seeking stability over growth.

insights.sentiment_reasoning: VDC demonstrates superior performance with significantly lower expense ratio (0.09%), broader diversification (105 holdings), stronger 1-year returns (0.05% vs -3.2%), lower max drawdown (16.55% vs 18.64%), and better 5-year growth ($1,244 vs $988 on $1,000 invested). Lower costs and proven track record of 21.9 years support positive outlook.

RSPS offers a differentiated equal-weighting strategy that reduces concentration risk and provides exposure to mid-sized companies, but is hampered by higher expense ratio (0.40%), negative 1-year returns (-3.2%), higher volatility (max drawdown 18.64%), and smaller asset base ($236.2M). Useful for risk-averse investors but underperforms on cost and returns.

Walmart is mentioned as the largest holding in VDC at 14.53%, representing significant concentration in the fund. No specific performance commentary provided; neutral mention as a major portfolio component.
Costco is the second-largest holding in VDC at 12.00%. Mentioned as part of the megacap concentration strategy; no specific performance analysis provided.
Procter & Gamble is the third-largest holding in VDC at 10.09%. Included as part of the market-cap weighted portfolio; no specific sentiment indicators provided.

items.title: Buffett's Departure From Berkshire Hathaway Puts Spotlight on Greg Abel
items.description: Warren Buffett has retired as the CEO of Berkshire Hathaway Inc., marking the end of his six-decade-long tenure. Greg Abel, his successor, took charge on the first day of the new year, stepping into a role fraught with challenges. What Happened: Abel’s key responsibility will be the effective allocation of Berkshire’s burgeoning cash reserves, which recently crossed the $350 billion mark.
